title: Word Search II
description: Given an m x n board of characters and a list of strings words, return all words on the board. Each word must be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once in a word.
constraints: board[i][j] is a lowercase English letter
words[i] consists of lowercase English letters
All the strings of words are unique
thinking_questions: Word Search I finds one word. Here you have multiple words — what's inefficient about running Word Search I for each?
If you build a Trie of all words and search the board against it simultaneously, what do you gain?
How do you prevent revisiting cells during a DFS path?

hints.L1: Build a Trie of all words. DFS from every cell, navigating the Trie simultaneously — prune when the current path isn't a valid prefix.
hints.L2: Build Trie with word end markers. DFS on board: at each cell, navigate Trie. Mark visited (e.g., '#'). If node.is_end, add word to result. Backtrack by restoring cell.

follow_up_questions: Why is building a Trie more efficient than running Word Search I for each word?
What optimization can you make to prune Trie nodes after a word is found?
Why do we mark cells as '#' and then restore — what bug does that prevent?
solution_notes: Trie of all words + DFS on board simultaneously. Mark/restore visited. O(m·n·4·3^(L-1)). Prune empty trie nodes for optimization.
